title-group.article-title: Inequalities in worsening work and income decrease/cessation in SARS-CoV-2 infection in adults and elderly people: the population-based SulCovid-19 study

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/05hpfkn88grid.411598.00000 0000 8540 6536Postgraduate Programme in Health Sciences, Federal University of Rio Grande, Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil
2https://ror.org/05msy9z54grid.411221.50000 0001 2134 6519Postgraduate Program in Nutrition and Food, Federal University of Pelotas, Pelotas, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil
3https://ror.org/05hag2y10grid.412369.b0000 0000 9887 315XHealth and Sports Science Center, Federal University of Acre, Rio Branco, Acre Brazil
4State Health Department, Pelotas, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil
5https://ror.org/05msy9z54grid.411221.50000 0001 2134 6519Postgraduate Program in Nursing, Federal University of Pelotas, Pelotas, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To evaluate inequalities in the worsening of work and income decrease/cessation among adults and elderly people with COVID-19 in the extreme south of Brazil.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This is a cross-sectional study based on the SulCovid-19 study carried out with adults and elderly people infected with SARS CoV-2 from December/2020 to March/2021 in the municipality of Rio Grande, RS, Brazil. The outcomes were: “worsening of work” and “income decrease/cessation” following COVID-19. The independent variables were gender, skin color, income, education, health insurance, age, marital status, schooling, morbidities, hospitalization and long COVID.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: prevalence of worsening work was 33.7% (95%CI 31.8; 35.5), while prevalence of income decrease/cessation was 44.6% (95%CI 42.7; 46.6). Female individuals with income in dollars between US0.00 and US192.0 had 13.0% (95% CI 1.01; 1.27) and 22.0% (95% CI 1.02; 1.46) greater prevalence of losing work and were 22.0% (95% CI 1.12; 1.34) and 49.0% (95% CI 1.31; 1.70) more likely to suffer a decrease/cessation of their income. Individuals with long COVID had 46.0% greater prevalence (95% CI 1.27; 1.68) of worsening at work and 24.0% (95% CI 1.13; 1.37) greater prevalence of income decrease/cessation when compared to those without the disease. Individuals with 3 symptoms or more had 86.0% (95% CI 1.58; 2.20) greater prevalence of worsening work and 47.0% (95% CI 1.31; 1.65) greater prevalence of income decrease/cessation when compared to those without long COVID symptoms.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: There was inequality in the worsening of work and income decrease/cessation for individuals infected with SARS CoV-2. Among these individuals, females, lower income, low education and greater long COVID severity were the most affected.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The acute severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 infection (SARS-CoV-2) has rapidly expanded around the world, with profound public health impacts and unprecedented crises in the economy and labor markets. Globally, there were more than 6.9 million deaths from coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) and almost 768 million confirmed cases by August 2023 [1]. In Brazil this number exceeded 702,000 deaths and 37.5 million cases [1]. In order to contain the advance of COVID-19, non-pharmacological measures such as isolation and social distancing have been adopted, which have shown promise in reducing SARS-CoV-2 transmission [2–4].

Long-term effects are emerging aspects related to SARS-CoV-2 infection that have sparked the interest of new studies, since besides contributing to discussions regarding the persistence of symptoms, they can assess the medical, epidemiological and sociopolitical challenges posed by long COVID. It is estimated that between 10 and 20% of people who have had COVID-19, including among those with mild or even asymptomatic cases, develop long COVID-19, also known as post-acute COVID-19, post-COVID-syndrome, long-term effects of COVID, post-acute COVID syndrome, chronic COVID [5, 6]. This manifestation is composed of a set of signs and symptoms that appear up to three months after infection, which last at least two months, are not explained by an alternative diagnosis and compromise the daily activities and quality of life of affected people [5]. In Brazil, the magnitude of the problem is unknown, whereby the number of people affected and economic and social significance after infection remain uncertain [7].

In this sense, in addition to health-related symptoms, there are other types of consequences, such as increased unemployment, which hit people working in the informal sector and living in precarious neighborhoods, that is, with low and irregular income [8]. The average unemployment rate in Brazil in 2021 was 13.2%, the second highest in the time series of the survey which started in 2012 and is conducted by the IBGE, reflecting the labor market situation and a year of intense losses due to the pandemic [9]. In the quarter ended in April 2020, the unemployment rate increased by 1.3% points (p.p.) compared to the previous quarter (November to January) and 0.1 p.p. with respect to the same quarter of 2019 (February-April) [9].

Studies have shown that 53% of people in the world temporarily stopped working, 32% lost their jobs or companies and 50% had their wages cut [10, 11]. The most affected workers were female, younger, Black, with less education a more precarious situation in the market, unable to carry out their work remotely, as well as those working in the informal sector of the economy [12, 13]. Brazil was already a country with extreme inequality, ranking second in Latin America and eleventh globally [14]. With the COVID-19 pandemic and social distancing, it could be seen that a population’s socioeconomic level directly interfered with the way it faced this period, increasing unemployment numbers and consequently loss or decrease in income [12].

Some studies indicate that COVID-19 contagion and long COVID have greater effects among the most vulnerable [15], and the presence of residual symptoms can lead to greater difficulty in returning to work. In view of the above, the objective of this study is to evaluate inequalities in the worsening of work and income decrease/cessation in adults and elderly people with COVID-19 in the extreme south of Brazil.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This is a cross-sectional study with data from the “Health monitoring survey of adults and the elderly following SARS-CoV-2 infectionin Rio Grande - SulCovid-19”. This research was carried out in Brazil in a municipality located in the extreme south of the state of Rio Grande do Sul, 300 km from the capital Porto Alegre. Rio Grande has an estimated population of 212,900 inhabitants [16]. According to the Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics (IBGE) census (2010) the Human Development Index (HDI) is 0.744, a value close to that estimated for Brazil which was 0.727 for the year 2010. Regarding its economy, with a Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita of US 9,009.22 (2020), Rio Grande is in the 126th place among 497 municipalities in the state and 698th among Brazil’s 5,570 municipalities. With regard to the average monthly salary of formal workers (3.4 minimum wages), it comes in ninth place in the state and 60th among the Brazilian municipalities [16].

The study population consisted of individuals residing in the urban area of Rio Grande aged 18 years or over, with a positive COVID-19 test result proven by real-time reverse transcription–polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), symptomatic during infection and who were followed up by the municipality’s epidemiological surveillance service. A further inclusion criterion was for infection to have occurred between December 2020 and March 2021.

Exclusion criteria were individuals residing in the rural area of Rio Grande, people lacking cognitive conditions to answer the survey, people deprived of liberty, those with incomplete data (telephone and address) available at the municipal epidemiological surveillance service and those who did not reside in the municipality at the time of applying the questionnaire for data collection. In order to compose the study population, the city’s epidemiological surveillance service provided a list with the records of those infected, whereby it was possible to select 3,822 individuals after applying the inclusion and exclusion criteria.

Data collection was performed by previously trained interviewers, through telephone interviews using the REDCap program installed on tablets (available at https://www.project-redcap.org/), from July to November 2021. Five telephone contacts were made on alternate days and times. After these attempts, if the person could not be found, a standardized message was sent to those telephones that had an instant messaging application (WhatsApp). In situations where the participant answered the message, a telephone or face-to-face interview was scheduled, according to the preference of the interviewee. For individuals for whom attempts to make contact via telephone were not successful, up to three home visit attempts were made on different days and times to carry out the interviews face-to-face.

The instrument developed for data collection was the same for both telephone collection and at home collection and contained semi-structured questions, preferably based on standardized instruments. The questions were designed to collect information on socioeconomic status, health conditions, behavior and food security, symptoms of COVID during and after the acute phase, physical activity, functional capacity, musculoskeletal symptoms, fatigue and use of health services. The instrument was tested based on a pilot study carried out with individuals living in the neighboring municipality, São José do Norte, who met the inclusion criteria, but were infected prior to the study.

For quality control, 10% of the study population was randomly drawn to apply a reduced questionnaire with key questions to allow identification of errors and/or false answers, in addition to evaluating, through the Kappa’s Cohen index, agreement between the answers provided by the respondents on both occasions. This assessment was carried out by one of the data collection supervisors. For further information about the methodology, consult Saes et al., (2023) [17].

The study was approved by the Research Ethics Committee of the Federal University of Rio Grande as per Opinion No. 4.375.697/Certificate of Submission for Ethical Appraisal No. 39081120.0.0000.5324. Ethical principles were respected and all participants were informed about the research objectives and their agreement to participate in it. Agreement to the oral consent form was recorded in the telephone interviews and also in face-to-face data collection.

Two outcomes were assessed. The first was in relation to the worsening of work, where the following question was asked: “Do you think that your SARS-CoV-2 infection has affected your occupation or work?” (got much worse; got a little worse; stayed the same; got a little better; got a lot better). For the purposes of analysis, the answers to this question were dichotomized (stayed the same/improved and got worse). The second outcome was related to changes in income, where respondents answered the following question: “How did COVID-19 affect family income?” (remained without income; decreased a lot; decreased a little or remained the same). The answers were dichotomized for the purposes of analysis into (remained the same/remained without income, decreased a lot and decreased a little).

In order to characterize long COVID, a filter question was prepared: “Which of these symptoms did you present after the COVID-19?”. The response options were dichotomized into (yes/no) for the following symptoms: headache, shortness of breath, dry cough, productive cough, pain/discomfort in breathing, loss of taste, loss of smell, change in sensation, fatigue, sore throat, runny nose, nasal congestion, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, joint pain, muscle pain, memory loss, attention loss, and skin changes. If there was a “yes” answer to a symptom, a further question was asked: “Are you still having this symptom right now?” When the answer was yes, this made it possible to identify long COVID. The number of symptoms was defined using an ordinal variable (1;2;3 or more).

The independent variables were: gender (female/male); age (18–59 years/60 years or older); skin color (white/black/brown/indigenous); income in dollars (0-192/193–385/386–770/771 or more); marital status (married-living with a partner/single/widowed); schooling (0–8/9–11/12 years or more); health insurance (no/yes); morbidities (no/yes); hospitalization (no/yes) and long COVID (no/yes; and 1/2/3 or more symptoms).

With regard to data analysis, the study population was initially described using absolute and relative frequencies. The prevalence rates and the respective 95% confidence intervals of the outcomes were calculated, according to the socioeconomic variables. Then, crude and adjusted analyses were performed using Poisson regression with robust variance adjustment. The following adjustment variables were included: sex; age; marital status, income in dollars. Other adjustment variables were added for the analysis of the outcome with long COVID: morbidities and hospitalization.

Magnitude of inequalities was measured through the gender, skin color, income, schooling and health insurance variables, using the Slope Index of Inequality (SII), which represents the absolute difference in predicted values of a health indicator between more privileged and less privileged individuals in terms of inequality indicators, taking into account the entire distribution of the stratified through an adequate regression model. The SII is calculated as the difference, in percentage points, between the estimated values for the extreme groups of the stratification variable. Although the SII was designed based on linear regression, in general, logistic regression is more appropriate when it comes to indicators of coverage or prevalence of health outcomes, as it avoids linear predictions outside the expected range for proportions (from 0 to 100 ) [18]. The data collected were exported from Redcap and analyzed using Stata® 16.1.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 4,014 participants tested positive for COVID-19 in the period assessed, 3,822 of whom were eligible to participate in the study (192 were excluded due to incomplete data). After losses and refusals (631 and 272, respectively), 2,919 subjects (76.4% of those eligible) were interviewed (Fig. 1). Regarding the outcomes, 2,537 individuals answered the question about how it affected occupation or work, 2,827 answered the question about changes in income and 2,888 answered the question about long COVID symptoms. The study population was composed of 59.6% females, 77.9% subjects with white/yellow skin color, the majority (44.2%) had 9 to 11 years of schooling, 60.6% were married/living with a partner, 62.6% had multimorbidity, 38.9% reported income between US193,00 and US385.00 dollars and 96.3% had not been hospitalized and 52.8% had health insurance (Table 1).

caption.p: Recruitment and exclusion process of individuals infected by SARS-CoV-2. Sulcovid-19 Survey. 1Excluded due to lack of information such as address and telephone. 2Individuals who did not respond to the three telephone contacts and who were not found at home during home visits were considered losses. Individuals whose address could not be found were also considered lost. 3 Individuals’ refusals included, among others, lack of time and interest in talking about their infection
Description of the sample of individuals after covid-19 in the City of Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul, 2021 (n = 2,919)

table-wrap-foot.p: aheart failure, cardiomegaly; arthritis, arthrosis, rheumatism or osteoporosis; asthma, bronchitis, emphysema or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
bhospitalization in nursery or intensive care unit

sec.p: Prevalence of worsening work was 33.7% (95%CI 31.8; 35.5), while prevalence of income decrease/cessation was 44.6% (95%CI 42.7; 46.6). Table 2 presents the crude and adjusted prevalence ratios. In the adjusted analysis, it was found that female individuals were 1.13 (95%CI 1.01; 1.27) times more likely to have work worsen and 1.22 (95%CI 1.12; 1.34) times more likely to quit /decrease your income compared to males. In the case of those with income between US$0.00 and US$192.00, the prevalence of worsening work was 1.22 times (95%CI 1.02; 1.46) higher and the prevalence of a change in income was 1.49 (95%CI % 1.30; 1.70) times higher, in relation to individuals with income above US$771.00. For individuals of black/brown/indigenous color and who had 0 to 8 years of schooling, a significant association occurred only in relation to income variation, with prevalence of 1.12 (95%CI 1.02; 1.22) and 1.31 (95%CI % 1.15; 1.51) times higher in terms of being without income or having their income reduced compared to white individuals and those with 12 years or more of education, respectively. People with health insurance had a 0.78 (95% CI 0.70; 0.88) times lower prevalence of worsening employment compared to those without insurance. With regard to being without income or having reduced income, there is a 0.69 (95% CI 0.64; 076) times lower prevalence of job deterioration compared to those who did not have health insurance.

caption.p: Prevalence and crude and adjusted analysis worsening of work and changes in income and relationship between socioeconomic variables and long COVID in individuals after covid-19 in Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil, 2021. (n = 2,919)

table-wrap-foot.p: *Adjusted for sex, age, marital status, income, morbidities and hospitalization

sec.p: Regarding presence of long COVID, we found that the prevalence of work worsening among individuals who had long COVID was 46.0% higher (95% CI 1.27; 1.68) when compared to those without long COVID. With regard to the effect of long COVID on income, we identified that the prevalence of income decreasing or ceasing was 24.0% higher (95%CI 1.13; 1.37) among respondents with long COVID when compared to those without long COVID (Table 2).

As for the relationship between the outcomes and the severity of long COVID, measured through the number of symptoms, there was a progressive increase in the prevalence of work worsening among individuals with 2 and 3 symptoms, going from 26.0% (95%CI 1.0; 1.56) among those with 2 symptoms to 86.0% (95%CI 1.58; 2.20) among those with 3 symptoms or more. With regard to income change, the results show that individuals with 3 or more long COVID symptoms were 47.0% (95%CI 1.31%; 1.65) more likely to be without income or to have decreased income (Fig. 2).

caption.p: Relationship between the outcomes and number of symptoms of long COVID in individuals after covid-19 in Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul Brazil, 2021. (n = 2,919)

sec.p: In relation to magnitude of socioeconomic inequalities of the outcomes “worsening of work” and “income decrease/cessation”, the inequality was lowest for those individuals with health insurance, with − 35.7 p.p (95%CI -42.3; -29.2) (Table 3).

caption.p: Slope index of inequality (SII) worsening of work and Hanges in income according to sex, age, skin color, income, education, marital status health insurance in individuals after covid-19 in the City of Rio Grande. Rio Grande do Sul. Brazil. 2021 (n = 2,919)

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Prevalence of worsening work affected one-third of the respondents, while income decrease/cessation affected about half of the individuals. Inequalities in worsening work and income decrease/cessation during SARS-CoV-2 infection were identified, affecting to a greater extent women, the poorer, those of black/brown/indigenous skin color, and less educated individuals. As for long COVID, we found that individuals who presented these symptoms had higher probabilities with regard to both the worsening of work and income decrease/cessation outcomes, with a tendency for the magnitude of this relationship to increase according to the number of symptoms of the disease.

Lockdown measures, in whole or in part, have affected almost 2.7 billion workers, representing around 81% of the global workforce [19]. A study carried out in Burkina Faso, Mali and Senegal highlighted that, by the end of April 2020, one in four workers surveyed had lost their jobs and one in two workers in those countries had suffered a drop in earnings as a direct consequence of the COVID-19 lockdowns [20]. According to the Brazilian National Household Sample Survey (PNAD-Covid19), carried out to measure the impacts of coronavirus, 13.5% of companies operating in Brazil reduced the number of their employees and, of these, 10.5% indicated a reduction of more than 50% of employees. Also according to the survey, the unemployment rate, which was 10.5% in early May 2020, reached 14.4% at the end of September of the same year. In addition, in November 2020, 19.6% of the employed had incomes below those normally received and in the state of Rio Grande do Sul 21.5% of the workforce was in this situation [21].

Individuals of black/brown or indigenous skin color had the greatest reduction in income. These data are in line with those presented by the PNAD-Covid19, which also indicate that people of black or brown skin color and those with less education were the most negatively affected by the economic impacts of COVID-19 [21]. Another study carried out in Brazil shows that the proportion of people of black and brown skin color who lost their jobs and were without income reached 18.0% at the beginning of the pandemic, contrasting with 14% of those of white skin color [11]. In the US, a study conducted by Chopra et al., (2022), 62.7% of the respondents were of black/brown skin color and 36.7% of these reported at least a slight financial impact and approximately 10.0% of them said they needed to use most of their savings [22]. Historically, it is known that people of black, brown and indigenous skin color already suffer greater social disadvantages, have more informal jobs, without a formal contract, showing that the COVID-19 pandemic ended up making these inequalities even worse [11, 23].

The economic crisis introduced by the new pandemic further deepened some of the inequalities already observed in the labor market, since those who were at a disadvantage had the worst indicators [11]. The effects of the COVID-19 pandemic affected companies suddenly, especially small businesses, which had an 88% drop in their revenues, exacerbating the already existing problems of lack of employment [24]. In Brazil, 37 million people living in informality and who do not have any social and labor rights were affected by the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic [8]. It should be noted that education levels have been continuously falling since 2015, and half of the jobs at the beginning of 2020 were informal, with risk of being left without income in this new scenario [13].

Job losses and overload were more intense during the pandemic among women. While the number of employed men fell by 0.9% in 2020, the reduction among women was 2.9%, and of the 82,300 jobs lost so far, 71.9% were held by women [25]. Women were more affected in terms of work worsening and income decrease/cessation, in addition to working away from home, women still did domestic activities and with the worsening of the pandemic, schools were closed, leaving women to take full-time care of their children [11].

Low education also predominated among the individuals most affected by the pandemic. According to a study by Barbosa et al., (2020), with the arrival of the crisis, having incomplete high school education or less means a 15% chance of transitioning into unemployment or out of the labor force [11]. Even before the pandemic these workers with low schooling were already characterized by informal jobs and lower wages.

In our study, having health insurance was a protective factor for both outcomes. Studies show that having health insurance is associated with higher education and better social conditions [26, 27], which may explain our results. A study in the United Kingdom found 75.0% prevalence of worsening work, two-thirds of the respondents had to stop working and more than a third reported loss of income [28]. The study by Zhang and Davis et al., (2021 and 2022) showed results along the same lines, whereby 45.6% of respondents reduced their working hours and 23.3% were not working at the time of the survey as a direct result of their illness [29, 30]. Reasons for change in work included sick leave, disability leave, resignation and inability to find a job that was in keeping with their new condition [30].

A cohort of patients in post COVID rehabilitation showed that just over half of the population (53%) returned to the same job and that 93% of those who returned required an adapted role in order to continue working [31]. Long COVID, despite not being a disability, can lead to disability because of many of its debilitating symptoms which are number dependent, i.e. the more symptoms post COVID the poorer the quality of health, causing the person to lose their social identity and consequently their work and income [29, 32].

The present study brings relevant and unprecedented information about worsening of work and income decrease/cessation and economic and health disparities with individuals affected by COVID-19 and long COVID. However, there are some limitations, a cross-sectional study cannot establish a cause-and-effect relationship, and there are no other investigations with the same population on the topic for comparison. The effects on income change and work worsening of asymptomatic patients were not investigated, which may result in underestimation of the proportion of the indicators. However, this limitation was minimized by the large study population size and sample calibration. More follow-up surveys are needed for more continuous monitoring of the long-term effects of the COVID-19 pandemic.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Based on the results, it was possible to identify inequalities in the worsening of work and income decrease/cessation of individuals infected by SARS CoV-2. Those most affected by this condition were females and those of black/brown/indigenous skin color, with lower education and lower income. Thus, the study made evident inequality in work and income among the most vulnerable. In addition, individuals identified as having long COVID and who suffered more severely from the disease, were also the most affected in relation to the outcomes. In this sense, this study can contribute to the adoption of social protection measures to minimize the financial effects caused among this population and will enable researchers and the government to gain greater awareness and knowledge about the current situations of the impacts of long COVID and its disproportionate socioeconomic impact. These results can be used as the basis for realigning current programs and spreading efforts to incorporate equity into many health and social policy decisions.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: YPV was financed by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior– Brasil (CAPES) case number 88887.605391/2021-00.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: All authors contributed to the study concept and design. YPV and KPM performed all statistical analyses. MOS supervised the study. YPV, LSC, ESA, JDMN, and LLJ wrote the article. YPV, KPM, TNG, RGN, SMSD and MOS revised the final version. All authors contributed to interpretation of data. All authors contributed to drafting the paper. All authors revised the text for intellectual content and have read and approved the final version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Rio Grande do Sul (FAPERGS), Brazil (grant number: 21/2551-0000107-0), SUS Research Program: Shared Management in Health (PPSUS).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This research involves human participants and was carried out in accordance with the relevant guidelines and regulations of the Declaration of Helsinki and this study protocol was approved by the Ethics Committee in Health Research of the Federal University of Rio Grande (Certificate of Submission for Ethical Evaluation nº 39081120.0 0.0000.5324). This research complied with the specific resolution of the National Health Council (466/2012) and informed consent was obtained from all subjects in accordance with the resolution of the Free and Informed Consent Term of the National Health Council.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
